text: The robotic surgical system is used to repair bilateral incarcerated inguinal hernias. The left side hernia appears relatively small in size.

The robotic surgical procedure involves working from lateral to medial, moving from Bogros space towards the supravesicular space. The right side shows both a direct and small indirect hernia, forming a pantaloon hernia, while the left side reveals a small indirect hernia with a small cord lipoma.
Believe it or not, he didn't really have a lot of pain when he was on the ice, but he was having pain when he was just standing for long periods of time.
The surgeon carefully maintains a thin flap during the robotic repair, revealing both a small indirect hernia and a small direct hernia.

I always take it down as one flap. Again, in the beginning I was doing two separate flaps, you know, leaving it separated and connected in the center, but I always find it better to place the mesh overlap in the center and I can have plenty of access to that area so I'm not manipulating needles around a central portion of a flap that's relatively easy to close.
The surgeon manipulates the anti-sac, visible as a white structure in the background, carefully stripping the surrounding fat tissue away from it.

The surgeon pulls on the inferior portion of the flap, carefully avoiding the hernia sac. The camera is positioned at a lateral oblique angle, showing the surgeon using cautery to dissect the tissue, with visible upward and medial traction applied.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the corner, maintaining a high and medial position relative to the iliac vein, while stripping down the medial portion of the hernia sac from the direct angle hernia space.
The surgeon navigates around the corner of the surgical field, staying close to the vas deferens. Structures medial to the vas and iliac vein are visible and accessible for manipulation.
The surgeon begins medial dissection towards the contralateral side, ensuring clearance past the symphysis pubis. The obturator plug is pulled down, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects the flap on the left side, maintaining a thin layer while pushing the fat laterally to cover the nerves. The dissection progresses towards the deep internal ring.
The surgeon exposes a wide and weakened internal ring, revealing a small indirect inguinal hernia. A small cord lipoma is also visible adjacent to the hernia.
